name: Sole F80 Folding Treadmill

specs.Connectivity: Bluetooth audio, USB charging, device holder

whatWorks: The 22" × 60" belt is wide and long enough for serious runners with long strides — most treadmill alternatives at this price use a 20" × 55" belt that feels cramped at 10+ MPH. This is the spec that separates the Sole F80 from mid-range competition.

The 3.5 CHP motor runs remarkably quiet under sustained load. Owners report holding 8–10 MPH for 30+ minutes with no heat buildup or speed fluctuation, and apartment-dwellers note the vibration is lower than expected for the speeds involved.
375 lb weight capacity is among the highest in home treadmills under $2,000 CAD, and the machine delivers on it — the frame doesn't flex noticeably under heavier users at speed.
The Cushion Flex Whisper Deck reduces joint impact versus running on pavement or cheaper treadmills, and owners who switched from a gym treadmill report less knee fatigue during equivalent workouts.
worthKnowing: At ~$1,799 CAD, this is a significant purchase — and it's heavy at 280 lb, meaning once it's placed it stays placed. Measure doorways and confirm floor loading before ordering; white-glove delivery is worth the cost.
The 9" LCD display is functional but not modern by 2026 standards — no touchscreen, no built-in content platform. Buyers who want streaming workout classes will need to mount a separate tablet.
Assembly requires two people minimum — the frame halves are heavy and aligning the bolt patterns solo is not realistic. Budget 2–3 hours with help.

No subscription-based content platform is included or required, which is a plus for buyers who don't want recurring costs — but a minus for buyers who want guided workout experiences without buying a separate device.

commonComplaints.title: Very expensive upfront cost…
commonComplaints.body: At $1,800 it's not an impulse purchase, and you need to commit to a permanent spot for it because it's not going anywhere once assembled. Great machine but sticker shock is real.

commonComplaints.title: Display looks outdated compared to competition…
commonComplaints.body: The screen is functional but it looks like it's from 2015. My Peloton-owning friends laugh at it. At this price I expected something more modern.

commonComplaints.title: Definitely a two-person assembly job…
commonComplaints.body: You cannot assemble this alone. I tried and couldn't hold the motor housing and torque the bolts at the same time. Get help or pay for professional assembly.

commonComplaints.title: Heavy and hard to move afterward…
commonComplaints.body: 280 lbs means you pick a spot and that's where it lives. I had to disassemble partially to move it 10 feet when we rearranged furniture. Plan your placement carefully.

verdict: The Sole F80 is the treadmill serious home runners actually buy after spending time on cheaper alternatives that vibrate, slow under load, or have belts too narrow for a full stride. The 22"×60" belt and 3.5 CHP motor at whisper-quiet operation are the differentiators — no competitor at this price point matches both. The dated display and the weight and price are real trade-offs. For buyers who want a no-subscription, buy-once-use-for-a-decade running machine, the F80 is the clear answer.

realBuyers.quote: I run a 10-minute mile pace for an hour and the belt never skips, the motor never whines, and the whole machine stays put. It's the best treadmill I've ever owned.
My neighbours have no idea I'm running at 9 MPH upstairs. The motor is shockingly quiet.
At 340 lbs I've used this daily for a year. Zero flex, zero mechanical problems. It just works.
The Sole F80 vs the NordicTrack isn't even close for build quality. This thing feels like a commercial machine.

sources.title: Sole F80 Review — Garage Gym Reviews

metaDescription: Sole F80 treadmill review for Canada: 22"×60" belt, 3.5 CHP motor, 375 lb capacity. Honest on price and dated display. ~$1,799 CAD.
introText: The Sole F80 is a premium folding treadmill built for serious home runners — with a 22"×60" belt, 3.5 CHP motor, and 375 lb weight capacity. It skips the subscription content platforms and focuses entirely on mechanical quality. Available on Amazon.ca (ASIN B00BX99NM8) at around $1,799 CAD.
